stimuli.description: Between-block attention check prompt asking the participant to press a specific key or alphabet-position-derived letter.
Color word ('red'/'blue'/'green') rendered in matching ink color (e.g., 'red' in #FF7070).

detection.selector: (()=>{const m=document.querySelector('.stroop-stim');if(!m)return false;const word=(m.textContent||'').trim().toLowerCase();if(['red','blue','green'].indexOf(word)<0)return false;const cs=window.getComputedStyle(m);const nums=(cs.color||'').match(/\d+/g);if(!nums||nums.length<3)return false;const r=+nums[0],g=+nums[1],b=+nums[2];let color=null;if(r===255&&g===112&&b===112)color='red';else if(r===112&&g===112&&b===255)color='blue';else if(r===112&&g===255&&b===112)color='green';return color!==null&&color===word;})()

response.response_key_js: (()=>{if(typeof window.correctResponse!=='undefined')return window.correctResponse;const m=document.querySelector('.stroop-stim');if(!m)return null;const cs=window.getComputedStyle(m);const nums=(cs.color||'').match(/\d+/g);if(!nums||nums.length<3)return null;const r=+nums[0],g=+nums[1],b=+nums[2];const isRed=(r===255&&g===112&&b===112);const isBlue=(r===112&&g===112&&b===255);const isGreen=(r===112&&g===255&&b===112);const gi=(window.efVars&&typeof window.efVars.group_index==='number')?window.efVars.group_index:1;let redKey,blueKey,greenKey;if(gi<=2){redKey=',';blueKey='.';greenKey='/';}else if(gi<=5){redKey=',';blueKey='/';greenKey='.';}else if(gi<=8){redKey='/';blueKey=',';greenKey='.';}else if(gi<=11){redKey='/';blueKey='.';greenKey=',';}else{redKey='.';blueKey='/';greenKey=',';}if(isRed)return redKey;if(isBlue)return blueKey;if(isGreen)return greenKey;return null;})()

stimuli.description: Color word ('red'/'blue'/'green') rendered in a non-matching ink color (e.g., 'red' in #70FF70).

detection.selector: (()=>{const m=document.querySelector('.stroop-stim');if(!m)return false;const word=(m.textContent||'').trim().toLowerCase();if(['red','blue','green'].indexOf(word)<0)return false;const cs=window.getComputedStyle(m);const nums=(cs.color||'').match(/\d+/g);if(!nums||nums.length<3)return false;const r=+nums[0],g=+nums[1],b=+nums[2];let color=null;if(r===255&&g===112&&b===112)color='red';else if(r===112&&g===112&&b===255)color='blue';else if(r===112&&g===255&&b===112)color='green';return color!==null&&color!==word;})()

response.response_key_js: (()=>{if(typeof window.correctResponse!=='undefined')return window.correctResponse;const m=document.querySelector('.stroop-stim');if(!m)return null;const cs=window.getComputedStyle(m);const nums=(cs.color||'').match(/\d+/g);if(!nums||nums.length<3)return null;const r=+nums[0],g=+nums[1],b=+nums[2];const isRed=(r===255&&g===112&&b===112);const isBlue=(r===112&&g===112&&b===255);const isGreen=(r===112&&g===255&&b===112);const gi=(window.efVars&&typeof window.efVars.group_index==='number')?window.efVars.group_index:1;let redKey,blueKey,greenKey;if(gi<=2){redKey=',';blueKey='.';greenKey='/';}else if(gi<=5){redKey=',';blueKey='/';greenKey='.';}else if(gi<=8){redKey='/';blueKey=',';greenKey='.';}else if(gi<=11){redKey='/';blueKey='.';greenKey=',';}else{redKey='.';blueKey='/';greenKey=',';}if(isRed)return redKey;if(isBlue)return blueKey;if(isGreen)return greenKey;return null;})()

attention_check.response_js: (()=>{const el=document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus');if(!el)return null;const text=(el.textContent||'').toLowerCase();const km=text.match(/press the ([a-z]) key/);if(km)return km[1];if(text.indexOf('third to last')>=0)return 'x';if(text.indexOf('second to last')>=0)return 'y';if(text.indexOf('last letter')>=0)return 'z';if(text.indexOf('first letter')>=0)return 'a';if(text.indexOf('second letter')>=0)return 'b';if(text.indexOf('third letter')>=0)return 'c';return null;})()

task_specific.rationale: Key mapping is counterbalanced via window.efVars.group_index (0-14, partitioned into 5 distinct red/blue/green -> {',', '.', '/'} assignments). Correct key is resolved at runtime per trial from the live ink color (computed via getComputedStyle and RGB literal match against #FF7070/#7070FF/#70FF70) crossed with the participant's group_index. Stimulus is visible for 1000ms; keyboard listener is active for the full 1500ms trial window with response_ends_trial=false. Fixation is 500ms; ITI is a decaying exponential mean 0.5s bounded [0,5]s.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian fits to congruent Stroop trials in young adults yielded mu in the 480-520 ms range.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Young-adult congruent ex-Gaussian mu averaged ~500 ms with sigma ~50 ms and tau ~85 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma for congruent trials clustered between ~40 and 60 ms in young adults.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Sigma for the young-adult congruent condition was ~45-55 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Tau for congruent Stroop in young adults occupied the 75-110 ms range.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Congruent tau averaged ~85 ms in the young-adult group.

congruent.rationale: Color-word Stroop with healthy adults typically yields mean RT ~600-620ms for congruent trials. Heathcote, Popiel & Mewhort (1991) and Spieler, Balota & Faust (1996) fit ex-Gaussians to Stroop with mu ~490-520, sigma ~45-60, tau ~80-110 for the congruent condition; mu+tau ≈ mean RT (~605ms here). Three-key version is slightly slower than 2-key, so values sit near the upper end of the canonical 2-key range.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Incongruent mu was modestly elevated relative to congruent, with most studies showing a +10-30 ms mu shift on top of a larger tau shift.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.table_or_figure: Section: Magnitude of the Stroop effect
citations.quote: Color-word Stroop interference in healthy adults averages roughly 50-100 ms across studies.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Sigma was modestly larger in the incongruent condition (~55-65 ms) than congruent in young adults.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma differences between congruent and incongruent are small but consistently positive in the incongruent direction.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: The Stroop interference loaded primarily into tau: incongruent tau exceeded congruent tau by ~25-40 ms in young adults.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Incongruent tau ranged ~110-150 ms, exceeding congruent tau by ~25-45 ms across participants.

incongruent.rationale: Stroop interference for healthy adults averages ~55ms (Heathcote 1991; MacLeod 1991 meta-analysis). The interference is asymmetrically loaded into tau (the exponential tail) per Spieler 1996 / Steinhauser & Hubner 2009 — incongruent slows the slow-tail responses more than the modal peak. Mu shifts +20ms, sigma +5, tau +35 relative to congruent, giving mean ~660ms and a Stroop effect of ~55ms.

autocorrelation.rationale: Trial-to-trial RT autocorrelation in short (~10 min) Stroop sessions is small and not consistently reported in the canonical Stroop literature. Leaving disabled to avoid imposing a structure not documented for this paradigm.

citations.authors: Lim, J., & Dinges, D. F.
citations.title: Sleep deprivation and vigilant attention
citations.table_or_figure: Review section on time-on-task RT drift
citations.quote: Time-on-task RT increases linearly for choice-RT tasks at rates on the order of tenths of a millisecond per trial in rested adults.

citations.authors: Sanders, A. F.
citations.title: Elements of human performance
citations.quote: RT drift in short choice-RT sessions is small and approximately linear over the first 10-15 minutes.

fatigue_drift.rationale: Across 124 trials (4 practice + 120 test), a small monotonic RT drift of ~0.1-0.2ms/trial (~15-25ms cumulative) is typical in choice-RT tasks of this duration (Sanders 1998; Lim & Dinges 2008 vigilance/fatigue review). Conservative because Stroop blocks are short and the literature for this specific paradigm does not document strong fatigue effects.

condition_repetition.rationale: Disabled to avoid double-counting with lag1_pair_modulation, which carries the documented Stroop congruency-sequence effect (the dominant lag-1 phenomenon in this paradigm). Pure stimulus-level congruency repetition without the asymmetric interaction is not separately documented for color-word Stroop.

pink_noise.rationale: The ex-Gaussian already supplies the bulk of within-condition RT variability; adding 1/f noise on top is not separately documented for short Stroop sessions and would inflate variance beyond published ex-Gaussian fits.

citations.authors: Gratton, G., Coles, M. G. H., & Donchin, E.
citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: Interference was reduced following incongruent trials and increased following congruent trials, producing the canonical congruency-sequence interaction.

citations.authors: Kerns, J. G., Cohen, J. D., MacDonald, A. W., Cho, R. Y., Stenger, V. A., & Carter, C. S.
citations.title: Anterior cingulate conflict monitoring and adjustments in control
citations.quote: Stroop RT on incongruent trials was ~20-30 ms faster when the preceding trial was also incongruent.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: The iI vs cI contrast in color-word Stroop is typically 20-40 ms; the cC vs iC contrast is somewhat smaller (10-20 ms).

citations.authors: Hommel, B., Proctor, R. W., & Vu, K.-P. L.
citations.title: A feature-integration account of sequential effects in the Simon task
citations.quote: Trials following errors were excluded from sequential analyses to avoid confounding congruency-sequence contrasts with post-error slowing.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Post-error trials are conventionally excluded from CSE analyses because post-error slowing inflates congruency-sequence estimates.

lag1_pair_modulation.rationale: The Gratton-style lag-1 congruency-sequence pattern is robustly documented in color-word Stroop: incongruent-following-incongruent is faster than incongruent-following-congruent, and congruent-following-incongruent is slower than congruent-following-congruent (Gratton, Coles & Donchin 1992; Kerns et al. 2004; Egner 2007 review). The interference reduction on iI trials (~20-30ms) is typically larger than the facilitation cost on iC trials (~10-15ms). skip_after_error=true follows the standard analytical convention of excluding post-error trials from sequence analyses (Hommel et al. 2004) since post-error slowing would confound the lag-1 contrast.

citations.authors: Rabbitt, P. M. A.
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: Responses on trials immediately following an error were systematically slower than baseline by approximately 30-60 ms.

citations.authors: Notebaert, W., Houtman, F., Van Opstal, F., Gevers, W., Fias, W., & Verguts, T.
citations.title: Post-error slowing: An orienting account
citations.quote: Post-error RT was elevated by ~30-50 ms relative to post-correct RT.

citations.authors: Danielmeier, C., & Ullsperger, M.
citations.title: Post-error adjustments
citations.quote: PES in conflict tasks including Stroop typically falls in the 20-60 ms range in healthy adults.

post_event_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing in color-word Stroop is well-documented at roughly 30-50ms (Rabbitt 1966 original; Notebaert et al. 2009; Danielmeier & Ullsperger 2011 meta-analysis). Range 25-55ms covers the central 70% of reported PES magnitudes for Stroop-class conflict tasks in healthy adults.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Training on integrated versus separated Stroop tasks: The progression of interference and facilitation
citations.quote: Stroop RT decreased by roughly 30-50 ms from block 1 to block 2-3 in healthy young adults.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Early practice produces the largest RT reductions, with most of the gain occurring within the first ~40-80 trials.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Training on integrated versus separated Stroop tasks: The progression of interference and facilitation
citations.quote: Most of the RT decline occurred by the second block; later blocks were essentially flat.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Training on integrated versus separated Stroop tasks: The progression of interference and facilitation
citations.quote: Block sizes in published Stroop designs typically range from 24 to 96 trials.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.table_or_figure: Power-law fits to choice-RT practice curves
citations.quote: Practice curves are well described by an exponential or power function with rapid decay over the first block and a long tail.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Training on integrated versus separated Stroop tasks: The progression of interference and facilitation
citations.quote: The block-to-block reduction was approximately exponential with most gain in the first transition.

practice_effect.rationale: Stroop RT shows a modest practice effect early in the session — typically ~30-50ms speedup from block 1 to block 2-3 in healthy adults (MacLeod 1998 practice review; Davidson et al. 2003). The 4-trial practice block is too short to fully absorb this, so block 1 of the test phase still carries some residual learning. Asymptote at block 2 with moderately fast decay (0.6) reproduces the typical pattern.

vigilance_decrement.rationale: Vigilance decrement requires sustained low-event-rate monitoring over 20+ minutes (Mackworth 1948; See et al. 1995). At ~10 minutes total task time with frequent decisions, the Stroop literature does not document a vigilance decrement. The mild end-of-session slowing is better captured by the enabled fatigue_drift mechanism.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject SD in mean Stroop RT for healthy adults is ~40-50ms (Hedge, Powell & Sumner 2018 reliability study; Salthouse & Meinz 1995). Stroop-effect-size SD across subjects is ~15-20ms (Hedge 2018). Sigma/tau scaling factor ~±15% reflects individual differences in distributional shape (Spieler 1996). Accuracy SD ~2-3% with a relatively narrow clip range (incongruent accuracy rarely below ~78% in healthy adults; the 3-key version is harder than 2-key so the lower bound is set below the canonical 2-key 85%). Omission rates are bounded ≤8% — higher would indicate disengagement rather than typical individual variation.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Between-subject SD in mean Stroop RT was ~40-55 ms across two large samples of healthy adults.

citations.authors: Salthouse, T. A., & Meinz, E. J.
citations.title: Aging, inhibition, working memory, and speed
citations.quote: Young-adult Stroop mean RT showed between-subject SD on the order of 40-50 ms.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of the Stroop effect (incongruent minus congruent) was ~15-20 ms.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.table_or_figure: Table 1 (between-subject scatter of sigma and tau)
citations.quote: Individual ex-Gaussian sigma and tau estimates vary by roughly ±15-20% around the group mean in young adults.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Accuracy SD across participants for Stroop was ~2-3 percentage points in healthy adult samples.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Omission rates in healthy adults on conflict tasks are typically below 3% with between-subject SD ~1-1.5%.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Incongruent Stroop accuracy in healthy adults rarely falls below ~85% on two-key versions; three-choice versions yield modestly lower floors.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Subject-level Stroop accuracy in their healthy adult sample ranged from ~80% to ~99%.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Participants with omission rates above ~8-10% were typically excluded as disengaged.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'Stroop RDoC (ExpFactory)' with 3 stimuli. Source files: jquery.min.js, bootstrap.min.js, math.min.js, jspsych.js, plugin-instructions.js, plugin-html-keyboard-response.js, plugin-fullscreen.js, plugin-survey-text.js, plugin-attention-check.js, experiment.j.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 2 conditions; enabled 4 temporal effects.
Produced 37 citations across 21 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 29 parameters; 7 are high-sensitivity.
